feedback.text: My feedback explains four reasons why this act should not be put forward. 1. Protecting EU citizens. This act if put forward, will destroy user experience of digital services in EU region. Typical user experience case as an example within the context of this act: Player plays a farming game where you grow carrots. When player has enough carrots, they can transfer those to in-game coins which then can be used to extend their farm. In the example, both carrots and in-game coins become digital currencies and when player plays the game, every carrot and coin usage which will happen in the sequence of seconds, triggers a popup UI or similar notification making the user experience practically impossible. 2. The act would create inequality among EU citizens Regions like China that have a very strict digital policies have created a gray economy where people start using VPN to use their daily digital services, so technically the act would not even work for everyone. If this act would be put forward, the exact same thing will happen in EU. The act would deliver inequality: you have people that know how to use VPN and dodge this act and people who doesnt. 3. The act will have negative effect on economy. This act would put offline most of the games that exists in EU. Destroying game industry in EU would have drastic negative effect in EU economy. Itd also generate brain leak to US and Asia as game developers would lose trust to EU decision making. 4. Foreign politics. Countries that have these sort of extremely strict digital acts are countries like China and North Korea. Is that the sort of group we like to join and voluntarily?
